what_it_does: Records a prioritized, five-item week-one product build plan for a channel/noticeboard application, scoped around verified user friction and a draft-queue capability.
observations: Title frames this as the entire defensible week-one build scope.

Five numbered priorities: fix Chrome/Tim MSG2 feedback UI issues, add a send-now button, build a draft queue with submit-all and count badge, add a replies view with unread badge, and fix the existing document switcher.

Item 1 cites Tim's MSG2 feedback as the only verified friction and includes specific UI changes: one-line top bar with hamburger + fitted title, icon buttons, growing one-line input, no on-screen echo of highlighted selection, PWA meta, remove persistent bottom bar, no emojis, use DNA brown-circle icons.
Item 3 is called the keystone above the rest because it closes the comment cycle.
Item 4 explicitly rejects chat/radial-menu designs in favor of a simple list with a badge.
Item 5 says fix the existing switcher rather than redesign it.

YAML front matter includes id, address, type=block, source=claude_code, state=current, title, author_session, channel=dragnet-development, a part_of link to board://item-f9f4a6a6, timestamps, and a filed history entry.
standouts: Every priority is anchored in either verified user feedback (Tim/MSG2) or the single workflow that closes the cycle (draft queue).
The draft queue is treated as the keystone feature above the other items.
Explicit scope exclusions: no chat, no radial menu, no emojis, no always-there bottom bar, no redesign of the document switcher.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item format with YAML front matter, id/address, type, source, state, timestamps, history, and a part_of link.
Uses a numbered markdown list with bold item headers.
Uses an assertive, opinionated planning voice (e.g., 'Do it first', 'That's it').
Breaks typical spec conventions by keeping the plan minimal and rejecting additional design work.
concerns: Item 1 bundles many distinct UI changes into a single priority, which could obscure individual acceptance criteria.
Relies on external context (Tim's MSG2 feedback) that is not included in the file.
No explicit owner, week boundaries, success criteria, or definition of done.
notes: A terse, feedback-driven week-one roadmap that defends a minimal five-item scope for a noticeboard/commenting tool.
questions: What is the full text of Tim's MSG2 feedback?
Which specific bugs exist in the current document switcher?
What are the 'DNA brown-circle icons' and where are they defined?
What is the parent item board://item-f9f4a6a6 and how does this plan relate to it?
Who owns each item and what defines 'done' within the week?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to define the entire defensible week-one build scope, and the file does exactly that by listing five prioritized items, so it matches.
apparent_themes: Cycle-closing workflow (draft queue → submit all → replies)
intention_signals: It is trying to constrain a larger project to a one-week, high-confidence slice by selecting only changes grounded in real user complaints and the one workflow that lets users collect and send comments in batches.
novelty: The deliberate framing of a whole week-one build as exactly five items, explicitly excluding common additions like chat, radial menus, and emojis, and justifying each item by whether it is real, tested, cheap or closes the cycle.
contribution: Provides a concise, decision-record-style scope statement that preserves the rationale for a lean week-one build and the specific UI/UX choices derived from user feedback.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard planning item that defines a five-priority week-one build for a commenting tool, emphasizing fixes from Tim's MSG2 feedback, a send-now button, a draft queue with submit-all, a replies list with unread badge, and a bug-fixed document switcher.
intention_for_embedding: To document and defend a minimal, feedback-driven week-one product scope for a channel/noticeboard application.
